10 Internal Fulfillment Engine - YouTube.mp4
This session focused heavily on customer-facing value creation activities. But what about the actions and activities of your internal teams, such as accounting, human resources, production, or engineering? Don't they add value too? Well, of course they do. In fact, these are all examples of internal fulfillment engines. With internal fulfillment engines, the customer is a member of the team. For example, the accounting team should create a fulfillment engine for delivering financials to the CEO in a timely and accurate manner. Similarly, the human resources team should create a fulfillment engine to assist a hiring manager in recruiting, hiring, and onboarding a new team member. The great thing about documenting internal fulfillment engines is that you're able to measure the true value and the true cost of your internal teams, which can be really helpful in determining where you should invest more resources, and where you should maybe look to outsource.
For example, a video editor might do amazing work, but if you know the same results can be achieved with a freelancer, or a contractor for less, it might make sense to outsource that particular internal fulfillment engine to a third-party service provider. Alternatively, you might choose to invest in this area or even spin it out into its own business unit if you discover that you have extra capacity, economies of scale, and particular skills in this particular area. The point is, you won't know unless you think of your internal services as stand-alone work centers. And the first step to doing that is to have them document their fulfillment engines while defining the customers that they serve. But having said all that... I don't want you to miss the big picture, all right?
So what I'm about to say is really, really important. Document internal fulfillment engines only after you have documented all of your critical customer-facing value engines. Let's face it, you're busy. Your team is busy. So focus on mapping the value engines that are going to have the biggest impact on your revenue, your profitability, and your customer's satisfaction. We highly recommend starting with your growth engines and then moving on to their associated fulfillment engines. Once you've done that, then you can shift to your innovation engines as well as your internal fulfillment engines. Again, this doesn't mean that the work these other team members are doing isn't important. We're simply starting with the customer and working backwards. And that is something that your customers and your finance department should appreciate.
